message:
fix(core): refresh memdb from retained write txn before prepared fast-path queries; add UNIQUE index perf probe

Fixes a memdb staleness bug where reads through prepared fast paths
(COUNT(*), COUNT+SUM, rowid lookup) could return stale counts after
a retained autocommit INSERT. The root cause: the retained-txn
commit path advanced the commit clock but deferred the memdb reload
to the next query. Fast-path queries that bypassed the normal VDBE
execution path never triggered the reload.

connection.rs — memdb refresh:
- advance_commit_clock_without_memdb_visibility(): new variant that
  increments the commit sequence counter without updating the memdb's
  visible-commit-seq, allowing the actual reload to happen inline
  while the retained transaction is still borrowed
- refresh_memdb_from_cached_write_txn_if_stale(): checks whether the
  cached write txn has published a commit-seq newer than memdb's
  visible-commit-seq; if so, reloads memdb from that txn. Called at
  the top of both query_with_params() and query_row_with_params()
  before the fast-path dispatch
- Retained autocommit path restructured: commit clock advances first,
  then memdb is reloaded inline from the retained txn. On reload
  failure, the txn is released (committed) and memdb falls back to a
  full pager reload with tracing::warn diagnostics
- Schema cookie/generation preserved across retained-txn reloads when
  no time-travel snapshot is captured (avoids spurious schema-changed
  errors on the next prepared query)
- SimpleCountIndexedRowidProbe fast path temporarily disabled (returns
  None) — the BTreeSet-based scan approach needs correctness fixes
  before re-enabling

New test:
- test_prepared_count_star_query_row_refreshes_from_retained_write_txn_before_fast_path:
  3 autocommit INSERTs via prepared statement, then prepared COUNT(*)
  via query_row — verifies the count sees all 3 rows

bd_1lsfu_1_operation_baselines.rs:
- New manual_perf_probe_write_10k_autocommit_prepared_unique_email_index:
  10K-row INSERT benchmark with a UNIQUE secondary index on email,
  comparing csqlite vs frankensqlite prepared-statement throughput
  (median of 3 runs, rows/sec)

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
